HackerOne Bug Bounty Dataset — Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Dataset: okweh1 — Publicly disclosed HackerOne vulnerability reports (2014–2025)
Records: 6,876 JSON files organised in year-based sub-folders
Goal: Load, clean, analyse and visualise the dataset to uncover patterns in the bug-bounty ecosystem.

Table of Contents¶

  1. Environment Setup
  2. Data Loading
  3. Data Cleaning & Feature Engineering
  4. Exploratory Statistics
  5. Visualisation — Volume & Temporal Trends
  6. Visualisation — Severity Analysis
  7. Visualisation — Bounty Analysis
  8. Visualisation — Weakness & Program Analysis
  9. Visualisation — Reporter Analysis
  10. Visualisation — Disclosure & Community Engagement
  11. Visualisation — Advanced / Cross-Feature Analysis
  12. Save Cleaned Dataset
  13. Key Findings Summary

1. Environment Setup¶

In [1]:
# Install dependencies (uncomment if needed)
# !pip install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn wordcloud scipy

import json, os, glob, warnings
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import seaborn as sns
from collections import Counter
from wordcloud import WordCloud
from scipy import stats

warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
%matplotlib inline

print("Libraries loaded successfully")
print(f"   pandas  {pd.__version__}")
print(f"   numpy   {np.__version__}")
print(f"   seaborn {sns.__version__}")

Libraries loaded successfully

In [3]:
# ── Global dark theme ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DARK   = "#0d1117"
CARD   = "#161b22"
ACCENT = "#58a6ff"
GREEN  = "#3fb950"
RED    = "#f85149"
YELLOW = "#d29922"
PURPLE = "#bc8cff"
ORANGE = "#f0883e"
COLORS = [ACCENT, GREEN, RED, YELLOW, PURPLE, ORANGE,
          "#79c0ff", "#56d364", "#ff7b72", "#e3b341"]

SEV_ORDER  = ["none", "low", "medium", "high", "critical", "unknown"]
SEV_COLORS = {"critical": RED, "high": ORANGE, "medium": YELLOW,
              "low": GREEN, "none": ACCENT, "unknown": "#8b949e"}

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor": DARK, "axes.facecolor": CARD,
    "axes.edgecolor": "#30363d", "axes.labelcolor": "#e6edf3",
    "axes.titlecolor": "#e6edf3", "text.color": "#e6edf3",
    "xtick.color": "#8b949e",   "ytick.color": "#8b949e",
    "grid.color": "#21262d",    "grid.alpha": 0.6,
    "legend.facecolor": CARD,   "legend.edgecolor": "#30363d",
    "font.family": "DejaVu Sans", "font.size": 11,
})

print("Plot theme configured")

Plot theme configured

2. Data Loading¶

The dataset lives in okweh1/ with one sub-folder per year (2014–2025) plus an ALL/ directory.
Each file is a single JSON object representing one disclosed vulnerability report.

We iterate every year folder, parse each JSON file and tag it with its year folder for later use.

In [5]:
# ── Set the base path to your local okweh1 folder ────────────────────────────
BASE_PATH = "D:\Axiler\Week1\Dataset EDA\okweh1"   # <-- change this if your folder is elsewhere

records = []

for year_dir in sorted(glob.glob(f"{BASE_PATH}/[0-9][0-9][0-9][0-9]")):
    year = os.path.basename(year_dir)
    json_files = glob.glob(f"{year_dir}/*.json")
    for fp in json_files:
        try:
            with open(fp, encoding="utf-8") as f:
                d = json.load(f)
            d["_year_folder"] = int(year)
            records.append(d)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  ⚠ Could not parse {fp}: {e}")

print(f"Loaded {len(records):,} records from {BASE_PATH}")

Loaded 6,876 records from D:\Axiler\Week1\Dataset EDA\okweh1
In [6]:
# ── File count per year ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
from collections import Counter

year_counts = Counter(r["_year_folder"] for r in records)
yr_df = pd.DataFrame(sorted(year_counts.items()), columns=["Year", "Files"])
yr_df["Cumulative"] = yr_df["Files"].cumsum()
print(yr_df.to_string(index=False))

 2020   1186        4113
 2021    913        5026
 2022    622        5648
 2023    444        6092
 2024    350        6442
 2025    434        6876

3. Data Cleaning & Feature Engineering¶

Each JSON has a rich nested structure. We extract the most analytically useful fields into a flat DataFrame, then engineer new derived columns.

Fields extracted¶

Field Source Notes
id, title, state, substate root Core identifiers
severity_rating severity.rating or root Normalised to lowercase
has_bounty, bounty_amount root Float; NaN when no bounty
vote_count, vuln_info_len root / computed Community signal + report length
created_at, disclosed_at root Parsed as UTC datetimes
reporter, reporter_cleared, reporter_verified reporter Reporter profile
team_handle, team_name, offers_bounties team Program info
weakness_name, weakness_id weakness CWE-style category
has_cve, cve_ids root CVE linkage
submitted_with_ai root AI-assisted flag

Engineered features¶

  • created_year, created_month, created_dow — temporal decomposition
  • disclosure_days — time (days) between submission and public disclosure
In [8]:
def extract_record(d):
    rep  = d.get("reporter") or {}
    team = d.get("team")    or {}
    tp   = team.get("profile") or {}
    sev  = d.get("severity") or {}
    weak = d.get("weakness") or {}

    bounty = d.get("bounty_amount")

    return {
        # ── identifiers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "id":                d.get("id"),
        "title":             d.get("title", ""),
        "state":             d.get("state", ""),
        "substate":          d.get("substate", ""),
        # ── severity ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "severity_rating":   (d.get("severity_rating") or sev.get("rating") or "unknown").lower(),
        # ── bounty ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "has_bounty":        bool(d.get("has_bounty?")),
        "bounty_amount":     float(bounty) if bounty else None,
        # ── community signal ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "vote_count":        d.get("vote_count", 0),
        # ── timestamps ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "created_at":        pd.to_datetime(d.get("created_at"),   utc=True, errors="coerce"),
        "submitted_at":      pd.to_datetime(d.get("submitted_at"), utc=True, errors="coerce"),
        "disclosed_at":      pd.to_datetime(d.get("disclosed_at"), utc=True, errors="coerce"),
        # ── reporter ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "reporter":          rep.get("username", ""),
        "reporter_cleared":  rep.get("cleared",  False),
        "reporter_verified": rep.get("verified", False),
        # ── program / team ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "team_handle":       team.get("handle", ""),
        "team_name":         tp.get("name", ""),
        "offers_bounties":   team.get("offers_bounties", False),
        # ── weakness ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "weakness_name":     weak.get("name", ""),
        "weakness_id":       weak.get("id"),
        # ── CVE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "cve_ids":           d.get("cve_ids", []),
        "has_cve":           bool(d.get("cve_ids", [])),
        # ── misc ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        "public":            d.get("public", False),
        "submitted_with_ai": d.get("submitted_with_assistant", False),
        "vuln_info_len":     len(d.get("vulnerability_information", "") or ""),
        "_year_folder":      d.get("_year_folder"),
    }

df = pd.DataFrame([extract_record(d) for d in records])
print(f"DataFrame shape: {df.shape}")
df.head(3)

DataFrame shape: (6876, 25)

3 rows × 25 columns

In [9]:
# ── Temporal feature engineering ─────────────────────────────────────────────
df["created_year"]    = df["created_at"].dt.year
df["created_month"]   = df["created_at"].dt.month
df["created_dow"]     = df["created_at"].dt.day_of_week   # 0 = Monday

df["disclosure_days"] = (df["disclosed_at"] - df["submitted_at"]).dt.days

print("New columns added: created_year, created_month, created_dow, disclosure_days")
df[["created_at", "disclosed_at", "disclosure_days"]].head(5)

New columns added: created_year, created_month, created_dow, disclosure_days

created_at disclosed_at disclosure_days

2 2014-07-03 05:06:17.257000+00:00 2019-11-12 09:44:13.020000+00:00 1958
3 2014-02-25 07:08:24.808000+00:00 2019-11-12 23:47:52.177000+00:00 2086
4 2014-08-21 18:21:11.762000+00:00 2019-10-14 08:48:43.922000+00:00 1879

4. Exploratory Statistics¶

Before plotting, we examine basic descriptive statistics, null rates, data types and value distributions.

In [11]:
# ── Data types & null rates ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Convert list-type columns to strings first so nunique() can hash them
df_hashable = df.copy()
for col in df_hashable.columns:
    if df_hashable[col].apply(lambda x: isinstance(x, list)).any():
        df_hashable[col] = df_hashable[col].apply(
            lambda x: ";".join(x) if isinstance(x, list) else x)

null_info = pd.DataFrame({
    "dtype":      df.dtypes,
    "null_count": df.isnull().sum(),
    "null_%":     (df.isnull().mean() * 100).round(2),
    "unique":     df_hashable.nunique(),
})
null_info

# ── Boolean flags summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
bool_cols = ["has_bounty", "has_cve", "public", "submitted_with_ai",
             "reporter_cleared", "reporter_verified", "offers_bounties"]
bool_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "True":  df[bool_cols].sum(),
    "False": (~df[bool_cols]).sum(),
    "True%": (df[bool_cols].mean() * 100).round(2),
})

5. Visualisation — Volume & Temporal Trends¶

Understanding when reports are filed reveals platform growth, researcher behaviour and seasonal patterns.

In [16]:
# ── Plot 1 · Reports per year (bar + trend line) ─────────────────────────────
yr = df[df["created_year"].between(2014, 2025)].groupby("created_year").size()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))
bars = ax.bar(yr.index, yr.values, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.85, zorder=3)
ax2  = ax.twinx()
ax2.plot(yr.index, yr.values, color=ORANGE, lw=2.5, marker="o", ms=6, zorder=4)
ax2.set_ylabel("Reports (line)", color=ORANGE)
ax2.tick_params(axis="y", colors=ORANGE)
ax2.set_facecolor(CARD)

for b in bars:
    ax.text(b.get_x() + b.get_width()/2, b.get_height() + 8,
            f"{int(b.get_height()):,}", ha="center", va="bottom",
            fontsize=9, color="#e6edf3")

ax.set_xlabel("Year"); ax.set_ylabel("Reports (bars)")
ax.set_title("📊 HackerOne Bug Reports Submitted Per Year", fontsize=14,
             fontweight="bold", pad=12)
ax.grid(axis="y", zorder=0); ax.set_xticks(yr.index)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [17]:
# ── Plot 2 · Monthly heatmap (Year × Month) ──────────────────────────────────
heat = (df[df["created_year"].between(2014, 2025)]
        .groupby(["created_year", "created_month"])
        .size()
        .unstack(fill_value=0))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 7))
sns.heatmap(heat, ax=ax, cmap="Blues", linewidths=0.4, linecolor="#21262d",
            cbar_kws={"label": "Reports"}, annot=True, fmt="d",
            annot_kws={"size": 8})
ax.set_xlabel("Month"); ax.set_ylabel("Year")
ax.set_xticklabels(["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
                    "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"])
ax.set_title("📅 Report Volume Heatmap (Year × Month)", fontsize=14,
             fontweight="bold", pad=10)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [18]:
# ── Plot 3 · Reports by day of week ──────────────────────────────────────────
DOW = {0:"Mon", 1:"Tue", 2:"Wed", 3:"Thu", 4:"Fri", 5:"Sat", 6:"Sun"}
dow  = df["created_dow"].map(DOW).value_counts().reindex(DOW.values())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
c_dow   = [GREEN if i < 5 else RED for i in range(7)]
bars    = ax.bar(dow.index, dow.values, color=c_dow, alpha=0.9)
for b in bars:
    ax.text(b.get_x() + b.get_width()/2, b.get_height() + 5,
            f"{int(b.get_height()):,}", ha="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Day of Week"); ax.set_ylabel("Reports")
ax.set_title("Reports Submitted by Day of Week\n(green = weekday, red = weekend)",
             fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.grid(axis="y"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

6. Visualisation — Severity Analysis¶

Severity drives bounty size, urgency and public interest. We examine its distribution and evolution over time.

In [19]:
# ── Plot 4 · Severity distribution (bar + pie) ───────────────────────────────
sev_counts = df["severity_rating"].fillna("unknown").value_counts()
sev_plot   = sev_counts.reindex([s for s in SEV_ORDER if s in sev_counts.index])
c_list     = [SEV_COLORS.get(s, ACCENT) for s in sev_plot.index]

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

bars = ax1.barh(sev_plot.index, sev_plot.values, color=c_list, alpha=0.9)
for b in bars:
    ax1.text(b.get_width() + 5, b.get_y() + b.get_height()/2,
             f"{int(b.get_width()):,}", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax1.set_xlabel("Count"); ax1.set_title("Severity Distribution (Bar)")
ax1.grid(axis="x")

wedges, texts, autotexts = ax2.pie(
    sev_plot.values, labels=sev_plot.index, colors=c_list,
    autopct="%1.1f%%", startangle=140,
    textprops={"color": "#e6edf3"}, pctdistance=0.82)
for at in autotexts:
    at.set_fontsize(9)
ax2.set_title("Severity Distribution (Pie)")

fig.suptitle("🛡️ Vulnerability Severity Breakdown", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [20]:
# ── Plot 5 · Severity rating trends over years (%) ───────────────────────────
sev_yr = (df[df["created_year"].between(2015, 2025)]
          .groupby(["created_year", "severity_rating"])
          .size()
          .unstack(fill_value=0))

for col in SEV_ORDER:
    if col not in sev_yr:
        sev_yr[col] = 0

sev_yr_pct = sev_yr.div(sev_yr.sum(axis=1), axis=0) * 100

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 6))
for sev in [s for s in SEV_ORDER if s != "unknown"]:
    if sev in sev_yr_pct:
        ax.plot(sev_yr_pct.index, sev_yr_pct[sev],
                marker="o", lw=2.2, label=sev.capitalize(),
                color=SEV_COLORS.get(sev, ACCENT))

ax.set_xlabel("Year"); ax.set_ylabel("% of Reports")
ax.set_title("📈 Severity Rating Trends Over Years (%)", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(loc="upper right"); ax.grid()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [21]:
# ── Plot 6 · Report detail length (chars) by severity ────────────────────────
vlen = df[df["severity_rating"].isin(["low","medium","high","critical"])].copy()
vlen = vlen[vlen["vuln_info_len"] < 10_000]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
for sev in ["low", "medium", "high", "critical"]:
    sub = vlen[vlen["severity_rating"] == sev]["vuln_info_len"]
    ax.hist(sub, bins=40, alpha=0.6, label=sev.capitalize(),
            color=SEV_COLORS[sev])

ax.set_xlabel("Vulnerability Info Length (chars)")
ax.set_ylabel("Reports")
ax.set_title("📝 Report Detail Length by Severity", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(axis="y")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

7. Visualisation — Bounty Analysis¶

Bounty data tells us what the industry pays for different vulnerability types and how economic incentives are structured.

In [22]:
# ── Plot 7 · Median bounty by severity + bounty amount distribution ──────────
bounty_df = df[df["has_bounty"] & df["bounty_amount"].notna() & (df["bounty_amount"] > 0)]
sev_bounty = (bounty_df.groupby("severity_rating")["bounty_amount"]
              .median()
              .reindex([s for s in SEV_ORDER
                        if s in bounty_df["severity_rating"].unique()]))

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

c2 = [SEV_COLORS.get(s, ACCENT) for s in sev_bounty.index]
ax1.bar(sev_bounty.index, sev_bounty.values, color=c2, alpha=0.9)
ax1.set_ylabel("Median Bounty (USD)")
ax1.set_title("Median Bounty by Severity")
ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x:,.0f}"))
ax1.grid(axis="y")

bounty_df = bounty_df.copy()
bounty_df["bounty_log"] = np.log1p(bounty_df["bounty_amount"])
ax2.hist(bounty_df["bounty_log"], bins=40, color=GREEN, alpha=0.85, edgecolor=CARD)
ax2.set_xlabel("log(1 + Bounty USD)"); ax2.set_ylabel("Reports")
ax2.set_title("Bounty Amount Distribution (log scale)")
ax2.grid(axis="y")

fig.suptitle("💰 Bounty Payment Analysis", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [23]:
# ── Plot 8 · Bounty rate by top programs ─────────────────────────────────────
prog_stats = (df.groupby("team_name")
              .agg(total=("id","count"), bounty=("has_bounty","sum"))
              .query("total >= 20"))
prog_stats["bounty_rate"] = prog_stats["bounty"] / prog_stats["total"] * 100
top_prog = prog_stats.nlargest(12, "total")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 6))
colors_bp = [GREEN if r >= 50 else YELLOW if r >= 20 else RED
             for r in top_prog["bounty_rate"]]
bars = ax.bar(top_prog.index, top_prog["bounty_rate"], color=colors_bp, alpha=0.9)
for b in bars:
    ax.text(b.get_x() + b.get_width()/2, b.get_height() + 0.5,
            f"{b.get_height():.1f}%", ha="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("Bounty Rate (%)"); ax.set_xlabel("Program")
ax.set_title("💵 Bounty Payment Rate — Top 12 Programs by Volume",
             fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.xticks(rotation=40, ha="right"); ax.grid(axis="y")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [24]:
# ── Plot 9 · Bounty amount vs community votes (scatter) ──────────────────────
sc = df[df["bounty_amount"].notna() & (df["bounty_amount"] > 0)].copy()
sc["sev_color"] = sc["severity_rating"].map(SEV_COLORS).fillna("#8b949e")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.scatter(np.log1p(sc["bounty_amount"]), sc["vote_count"],
           c=sc["sev_color"], alpha=0.55, s=25, edgecolors="none")
for sev, col in SEV_COLORS.items():
    ax.scatter([], [], color=col, label=sev.capitalize(), s=50)
ax.set_xlabel("log(1 + Bounty USD)"); ax.set_ylabel("Vote Count")
ax.set_title("💡 Bounty Amount vs. Community Votes", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(title="Severity", loc="upper left"); ax.grid()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

8. Visualisation — Weakness & Program Analysis¶

Which vulnerability types dominate? Which programs attract the most researchers?

In [25]:
# ── Plot 10 · Top 15 weakness types ──────────────────────────────────────────
top_weak = df["weakness_name"].value_counts().head(15)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 6))
colors_w = [COLORS[i % len(COLORS)] for i in range(len(top_weak))]
bars = ax.barh(top_weak.index[::-1], top_weak.values[::-1],
               color=colors_w[::-1], alpha=0.9)
for b in bars:
    ax.text(b.get_width() + 3, b.get_y() + b.get_height()/2,
            f"{int(b.get_width()):,}", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Number of Reports")
ax.set_title("🐛 Top 15 Vulnerability Weakness Types", fontsize=14,
             fontweight="bold", pad=10)
ax.grid(axis="x"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [26]:
# ── Plot 11 · Top 15 programs by report count ────────────────────────────────
top_teams = df["team_name"].value_counts().head(15)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 6))
colors_t  = [COLORS[i % len(COLORS)] for i in range(len(top_teams))]
bars = ax.barh(top_teams.index[::-1], top_teams.values[::-1],
               color=colors_t[::-1], alpha=0.9)
for b in bars:
    ax.text(b.get_width() + 2, b.get_y() + b.get_height()/2,
            f"{int(b.get_width()):,}", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Reports Received")
ax.set_title("🏢 Top 15 Bug Bounty Programs by Report Count", fontsize=14,
             fontweight="bold", pad=10)
ax.grid(axis="x"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [27]:
# ── Plot 12 · Word cloud of report titles ────────────────────────────────────
STOP_EXTRA = {"the","a","an","in","of","and","or","to","for","on","with",
              "is","at","by","this","that","it","via","from","using","how"}

text = " ".join(df["title"].dropna().str.lower().tolist())
wc   = WordCloud(width=1400, height=700, background_color="#0d1117",
                 colormap="Blues", max_words=200,
                 stopwords=STOP_EXTRA).generate(text)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 7), facecolor=DARK)
ax.imshow(wc, interpolation="bilinear"); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("💬 Word Cloud of Report Titles", fontsize=16,
             fontweight="bold", color="#e6edf3", pad=10)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

9. Visualisation — Reporter Analysis¶

A small cohort of prolific researchers drives a disproportionate share of all submissions — a classic power-law pattern.

In [28]:
# ── Plot 13 · Top 15 reporters by volume ─────────────────────────────────────
top_rep = df["reporter"].value_counts().head(15)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 6))
colors_r = [COLORS[i % len(COLORS)] for i in range(len(top_rep))]
bars = ax.bar(top_rep.index, top_rep.values, color=colors_r, alpha=0.9)
for b in bars:
    ax.text(b.get_x() + b.get_width()/2, b.get_height() + 1,
            str(int(b.get_height())), ha="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Reporter"); ax.set_ylabel("Reports Submitted")
ax.set_title("🏆 Top 15 Bug Reporters by Volume", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.xticks(rotation=40, ha="right"); ax.grid(axis="y")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [29]:
# ── Plot 14 · Reporter submissions — power-law check ─────────────────────────
rep_counts = df["reporter"].value_counts()

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

ax1.hist(rep_counts.values, bins=60, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.85, edgecolor=CARD)
ax1.set_xlabel("Reports per researcher"); ax1.set_ylabel("Researchers")
ax1.set_title("Distribution of Reports per Researcher"); ax1.grid(axis="y")

ax2.loglog(np.arange(1, len(rep_counts)+1), rep_counts.values,
           ".", color=PURPLE, alpha=0.6, ms=4)
ax2.set_xlabel("Rank (log)"); ax2.set_ylabel("Reports (log)")
ax2.set_title("Rank vs Reports (log-log) — Power Law?"); ax2.grid()

fig.suptitle("📉 Reporter Submission Power-Law Distribution",
             fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [30]:
# ── Cleared vs uncleared reporter stats ──────────────────────────────────────
cleared_stats = df.groupby("reporter_cleared").agg(
    reports       = ("id",            "count"),
    avg_votes     = ("vote_count",    "mean"),
    bounty_rate   = ("has_bounty",    "mean"),
    avg_bounty    = ("bounty_amount", "mean"),
).round(2)
cleared_stats.index = ["Not Cleared", "Cleared"]
print("Reporter Cleared vs Not Cleared:")
print(cleared_stats.to_string())

Reporter Cleared vs Not Cleared:
             reports  avg_votes  bounty_rate  avg_bounty
Not Cleared     5681      32.54         0.41     1480.35
Cleared         1195      58.14         0.55     2831.48

10. Visualisation — Disclosure & Community Engagement¶

Disclosure timelines and community votes reveal how the platform and programs behave in terms of transparency and researcher recognition.

In [31]:
# ── Plot 15 · Time from submission to disclosure ──────────────────────────────
disc = df["disclosure_days"].dropna()
disc = disc[(disc >= 0) & (disc < 1500)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
ax.hist(disc, bins=60, color=PURPLE, alpha=0.85, edgecolor=CARD)
ax.axvline(disc.median(), color=YELLOW, lw=2, ls="--",
           label=f"Median: {disc.median():.0f} days")
ax.axvline(disc.mean(),   color=RED,    lw=2, ls="--",
           label=f"Mean:   {disc.mean():.0f} days")
ax.set_xlabel("Days to Disclosure"); ax.set_ylabel("Reports")
ax.set_title("⏱️ Time from Submission to Public Disclosure",
             fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(axis="y"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [32]:
# ── Plot 16 · Disclosure time by severity (box plot) ─────────────────────────
disc_sev = (df[df["severity_rating"].isin(["low","medium","high","critical"])]
            .copy())
disc_sev = disc_sev[disc_sev["disclosure_days"].between(0, 1500)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
groups   = [disc_sev[disc_sev["severity_rating"] == s]["disclosure_days"].dropna()
            for s in ["low","medium","high","critical"]]
bp = ax.boxplot(groups, patch_artist=True, notch=False,
                medianprops={"color": DARK, "lw": 2})
for patch, sev in zip(bp["boxes"], ["low","medium","high","critical"]):
    patch.set_facecolor(SEV_COLORS[sev]); patch.set_alpha(0.8)
ax.set_xticklabels(["Low","Medium","High","Critical"])
ax.set_ylabel("Disclosure Days"); ax.set_xlabel("Severity")
ax.set_title("⏳ Disclosure Time Distribution by Severity",
             fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.grid(axis="y"); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [33]:
# ── Plot 17 · CVE-linked reports by year ─────────────────────────────────────
cve_yr = (df[df["created_year"].between(2014, 2025)]
          .groupby(["created_year","has_cve"])
          .size()
          .unstack(fill_value=0))
cve_yr.columns = ["No CVE", "Has CVE"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
cve_yr.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, ax=ax,
            color=[CARD, RED], edgecolor="#30363d", alpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlabel("Year"); ax.set_ylabel("Reports")
ax.set_title("🔗 CVE-Linked Reports by Year", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(axis="y"); plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

11. Visualisation — Advanced / Cross-Feature Analysis¶

Cross-feature plots, correlation analysis, AI submission trends and the summary dashboard.

In [34]:
# ── Plot 18 · AI-assisted vs human submissions (2022–2025) ──────────────────
ai = (df[df["created_year"].between(2022, 2025)]
      .groupby(["created_year","submitted_with_ai"])
      .size()
      .unstack(fill_value=0))
ai.columns = ["Human", "AI-assisted"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ai.plot(kind="bar", ax=ax, color=[ACCENT, ORANGE], alpha=0.9, edgecolor=CARD)
ax.set_xlabel("Year"); ax.set_ylabel("Reports")
ax.set_title("🤖 AI-Assisted vs Human Submissions (2022–2025)",
             fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(axis="y"); plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [35]:
# ── Plot 19 · Feature correlation heatmap ────────────────────────────────────
num_cols = ["vote_count","vuln_info_len","disclosure_days",
            "has_bounty","bounty_amount","has_cve",
            "reporter_cleared","reporter_verified"]
corr = df[num_cols].apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce").corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr, dtype=bool))
sns.heatmap(corr, ax=ax, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt=".2f",
            cmap="coolwarm", center=0,
            linewidths=0.5, linecolor="#21262d",
            cbar_kws={"label": "Pearson r"},
            annot_kws={"size": 10})
ax.set_title("🔥 Feature Correlation Heatmap", fontsize=14,
             fontweight="bold", pad=10)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [36]:
# ── Plot 20 · Summary KPI dashboard ──────────────────────────────────────────
total     = len(df)
w_bounty  = int(df["has_bounty"].sum())
med_bount = df["bounty_amount"].median()
uniq_rep  = df["reporter"].nunique()
uniq_prg  = df["team_name"].nunique()
med_disc  = df["disclosure_days"].median()
pct_crit  = (df["severity_rating"] == "critical").mean() * 100
pct_high  = (df["severity_rating"] == "high").mean()    * 100

kpis = [
    ("Total Reports",     f"{total:,}",            ACCENT),
    ("Unique Reporters",  f"{uniq_rep:,}",          GREEN),
    ("Unique Programs",   f"{uniq_prg:,}",          PURPLE),
    ("With Bounty",       f"{w_bounty:,}",          ORANGE),
    ("Median Bounty",
     f"${med_bount:,.0f}" if not np.isnan(med_bount) else "N/A", YELLOW),
    ("Median Disclosure",
     f"{med_disc:.0f} days" if not np.isnan(med_disc) else "N/A", RED),
    ("Critical Rate",     f"{pct_crit:.1f}%",       RED),
    ("High Rate",         f"{pct_high:.1f}%",       ORANGE),
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(16, 6), facecolor=DARK)
for ax, (label, val, col) in zip(axes.flat, kpis):
    ax.set_facecolor(CARD)
    ax.text(0.5, 0.58, val, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=22, fontweight="bold", color=col, transform=ax.transAxes)
    ax.text(0.5, 0.25, label, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=11, color="#8b949e", transform=ax.transAxes)
    for sp in ax.spines.values():
        sp.set_edgecolor(col); sp.set_linewidth(2)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

fig.suptitle("📊 HackerOne Dataset — Key Statistics Dashboard",
             fontsize=16, fontweight="bold", color="#e6edf3")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [37]:
# ── Statistical test: do cleared reporters earn more bounty? ─────────────────
cleared   = df[df["reporter_cleared"]  & df["bounty_amount"].notna()]["bounty_amount"]
uncleared = df[~df["reporter_cleared"] & df["bounty_amount"].notna()]["bounty_amount"]

stat, p = stats.mannwhitneyu(cleared, uncleared, alternative="two-sided")
print(f"Mann-Whitney U test — Cleared vs Un-cleared reporters on bounty amount")
print(f"  U-statistic : {stat:,.0f}")
print(f"  p-value     : {p:.4f}")
print(f"  Cleared   median bounty: ${cleared.median():,.2f}  (n={len(cleared):,})")
print(f"  Uncleared median bounty: ${uncleared.median():,.2f}  (n={len(uncleared):,})")
if p < 0.05:
    print("  → Statistically significant difference (α=0.05)")
else:
    print("  → No statistically significant difference (α=0.05)")

Mann-Whitney U test — Cleared vs Un-cleared reporters on bounty amount
  U-statistic : 113,746
  p-value     : 0.0678
  Cleared   median bounty: $560.00  (n=192)
  Uncleared median bounty: $500.00  (n=1,095)
  → No statistically significant difference (α=0.05)

12. Save Cleaned Dataset¶

We serialise the cleaned, enriched DataFrame to a single CSV file for downstream use.
List-type columns (cve_ids) are joined with ; for CSV compatibility.

In [38]:
# ── Serialise list columns & export ──────────────────────────────────────────
df_out = df.copy()
df_out["cve_ids"] = df_out["cve_ids"].apply(
    lambda x: ";".join(x) if isinstance(x, list) else "")

OUTPUT_PATH = "./hackerone_cleaned.csv"
df_out.to_csv(OUTPUT_PATH, index=False)
print(f"✅ Saved {len(df_out):,} rows × {len(df_out.columns)} columns → {OUTPUT_PATH}")

✅ Saved 6,876 rows × 29 columns → ./hackerone_cleaned.csv
In [39]:
# ── Quick sanity check on the saved file ─────────────────────────────────────
check = pd.read_csv(OUTPUT_PATH)
print(f"Re-loaded shape : {check.shape}")
print(f"Columns         : {list(check.columns)}")
check.head(3)

Re-loaded shape : (6876, 29)

Out[39]:
id title state substate severity_rating has_bounty bounty_amount vote_count created_at submitted_at ... cve_ids has_cve public submitted_with_ai vuln_info_len _year_folder created_year created_month created_dow disclosure_days
0 11073 XSS in gist integration Closed resolved unknown True 500.0 156 2014-05-06 18:21:27.236000+00:00 2014-05-06 18:21:27.236000+00:00 ... NaN False True False 262 2014 2014 5 1 1817
1 16910 Cross-site information assertion leak via Cont... Closed resolved unknown False NaN 13 2014-06-18 22:31:04.534000+00:00 2014-06-18 22:31:04.534000+00:00 ... NaN False True False 4323 2014 2014 6 2 1539
2 18843 use-after-free vulnerability in Flash Player Closed resolved unknown True NaN 1 2014-07-03 05:06:17.257000+00:00 2014-07-03 05:06:17.257000+00:00 ... NaN False True False 6175 2014 2014 7 3 1958

3 rows × 29 columns

13. Key Findings Summary¶

# Finding Detail
1 Peak activity in 2020 Reports peaked at 1,186 in 2020 then gradually declined — possibly reflecting platform maturation and increased duplicate filtering.
2 Weekend slowdown Researchers submit ~30–40 % fewer reports on Saturdays and Sundays, showing bug-hunting is largely a weekday activity.
3 Medium/High dominate severity Together these two bands account for >60 % of all reports; critical bugs are rare (<10 %).
4 High severity rising The proportion of high-severity disclosures has grown year-on-year since 2018.
5 Only ~19 % earn a bounty Many programs (e.g. U.S. Dept. of Defense) are prestige/non-monetary, dragging the bounty rate down.
6 XSS & Information Disclosure lead Classic web vulnerabilities dominate; IDOR and Access Control issues are growing.
7 Long disclosure timelines Median time to public disclosure exceeds 300 days — indicating slow internal patch cycles.
8 Power-law reporter distribution A handful of elite researchers submit tens of reports while the vast majority submit 1–2.
9 CVE linkage is rare Fewer than 5 % of reports are tied to a formal CVE, as most bugs are platform-specific.
10 AI-assisted submissions emerging A measurable and growing fraction of 2024–2025 reports are flagged as AI-assisted.

Next steps: NLP on vulnerability_information, time-series forecasting of submission volume, or building a severity predictor using weakness + program features.
